text:
We introduce a novel family of ruthenium complexes defined by the general formula [Ruⁿ(tda)(py)₂]ᵐ⁺ (where n = 2, 3, 4), utilizing the pentadentate tda²⁻ ligand ([2,2':6',2"-terpyridine]-6,6"-dicarboxylate). Among these, the species [Ruᴵⱽ(OH)(tda-κ-N3O)(py)₂]⁺ (4H⁺), formed via hydroxo coordination to the precursor 3²⁺ under basic conditions, emerges as an extraordinary water oxidation catalyst. Characterization of these complexes was achieved through an integrated approach using X-ray diffraction, spectroscopic methods (NMR, EPR, UV-vis), and electrochemical techniques (CV, DPV, coulometry), supported by DFT calculations.

Structural analysis reveals that the coordination environment of the Ru center is highly sensitive to its oxidation state: the Ru(IV) species is seven-coordinated and diamagnetic, whereas the Ru(II) analogue is six-coordinated with a distinctive unbonded "dangling" carboxylate group. The Ru(III) state occupies a structural middle ground between these two geometries. We demonstrate that 4H⁺ can be generated from 3²⁺ either potentiometrically or voltammetrically, with both species coexisting in solution at specific pH ranges.

While the parent complex 3²⁺ remains catalytically inactive, 4H⁺ exhibits world-record performance. Foot of the wave analysis (FOWA) reveals unprecedented turnover frequencies (TOF) of 8,000 s⁻¹ at pH 7.0 and up to 50,000 s⁻¹ at pH 10.0. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations elucidate the full catalytic cycle, identifying a water nucleophilic attack on a Ru(V) oxo species as the rate-determining step. Crucially, the calculations highlight the functional role of the dangling carboxylate, which acts as an internal base. This intramolecular proton transfer significantly lowers the activation free energy for O–O bond formation, providing a blueprint for the design of high-efficiency molecular catalysts.
